writing_type: NIH grant proposals (R01 and similar federal research funding applications)
user_goals_summary: This user writes grant proposals that build emotional and intellectual urgency through escalating narrative architecture, making reviewers feel the problem before encountering the proposed solution. They value extreme specificity — naming incumbents, naming what's displaced, naming the exact clinical reality — and they want every section to feel like a complete, independently valuable thought rather than a setup for what follows. Accessibility across disciplinary boundaries is essential, but never at the cost of scientific precision.

rubric.name: Escalating Urgency
rubric.description: Each sentence should raise the stakes beyond the previous one, building vertical pressure rather than cataloguing facts at equal weight and temperature. By the time the proposed work appears, reviewers should feel the problem is urgent enough that the solution feels inevitable — 'almost frustrated on behalf of patients.' The gap must be felt, not just named.

dimensions.label: Each successive sentence escalates stakes or narrows the problem rather than restating at the same level
The problem is rendered in terms that produce emotional engagement, not just intellectual understanding
The solution is withheld until sufficient pressure has been built — tension is not released by pivoting to the proposed work too early
By the time the proposed work is introduced, it feels like the only logical response rather than one of several possible approaches

rubric.name: Explicit Contrast and Displacement
rubric.description: The writing must name what currently fills the space the proposed work will occupy — the specific incumbent approach, the existing workaround, the thing being replaced — and articulate why it falls short as a design flaw, not a resource problem. Contrasts must be named and landed, not gestured at or implied. Abstractions like 'current methods are inadequate' must be replaced with concrete descriptions of what those methods actually look like in practice.

dimensions.label: The specific existing approach or workaround currently occupying the clinical/research slot is named concretely
The incumbent's limitation is framed as a design or structural flaw, not merely a resource or effort gap
The difference between the proposed approach and the incumbent is stated explicitly, not left for the reader to infer
Existing literature and prior approaches are reframed as inputs to or foundations for the proposed work, not simply as competitors or failures

rubric.name: Rhetorical Craft and Register Control
rubric.description: Sentence rhythm, length variation, and register should be deliberately controlled to serve the argument's emotional arc. Closing lines must feel like arrivals — doors opening — not summaries or process descriptions. Temperature should sit between bold conviction and quiet confidence: speaking on behalf of patients and stakes, not pivoting to methodology vocabulary. Process language ('builds a platform,' 'enables detection') is treated as a register collapse when it follows urgency-driven prose.

dimensions.label: Section and paragraph endings feel like promises or openings, not summaries or announcements of methodology
Sentence length and structure vary deliberately to control pacing — short sentences for impact, longer ones for building complexity
The prose maintains a consistent register throughout each section without collapsing from patient-centered urgency into bureaucratic or process language
Claims are bold enough to convey conviction but restrained enough to avoid sounding promotional or theatrical
Key images or phrases introduced early are called back later, making the prose feel architecturally shaped rather than accumulated

rubric.name: Interdisciplinary Accessibility
rubric.description: Writing must be legible to reviewers from different disciplinary backgrounds — neuroscience, engineering, public health, clinical medicine — without dumbing down the science. Technical terms should be included for precision but paired with brief contextual cues (parentheticals, appositive definitions) so non-specialist reviewers can follow the argument without losing the thread. Specificity should serve clarity: naming exact instruments, protocols, or signals helps non-experts understand what's broken or proposed more than abstractions do.

dimensions.label: Discipline-specific terms are accompanied by brief contextual cues that allow non-specialist reviewers to continue reading without confusion
Key arguments address concerns that matter to different reviewer backgrounds — e.g., feasibility for engineers, clinical relevance for clinicians, scalability for public health
Concrete names of instruments, protocols, or signals are used in place of vague references, because specificity aids rather than hinders comprehension
No section assumes deep familiarity with a single discipline's methodology without providing enough context for a reviewer from another field

rubric.name: Team Positioning as Launching Position
rubric.description: Investigator credentials and preliminary data should be framed as evidence of readiness and convergence — a specific threshold that only this team could be standing at — rather than as a résumé or track record. Past work is a launching position, not a credential. The reader should feel that this team is uniquely positioned to execute because of a specific sequence of prior work that converged at this moment, not merely because they are competent and experienced.

dimensions.label: Team qualifications are framed as evidence of being uniquely positioned to execute now, rather than as a list of past accomplishments
Preliminary data and team expertise are presented as converging toward a specific threshold that makes the proposed work newly possible
Investigator roles emerge from the scientific logic rather than appearing in a separate personnel section disconnected from the argument
Preliminary data serves as an active argumentative move (proving the approach is ready) rather than passive background information

rubric.name: Structural Resilience of Aims
rubric.description: Each aim should feel like a complete thought that produces a usable artifact — a framework, methodology, or characterized dataset — with independent value, not just a setup for subsequent aims. The relationship between aims should be one of building and refinement, not hard dependency. Failure mode awareness should be conveyed through language that signals intellectual flexibility and confidence, not defensiveness or hedging.

dimensions.label: Each aim explicitly names a deliverable that has independent scientific or clinical value regardless of outcomes in other aims
The primary deliverable is framed as a reusable framework or methodology, not solely as a binary finding that either works or doesn't
The aim's value is decoupled from any single component's performance — it retains value even if individual elements underperform
Language about contingencies and adaptability conveys intellectual maturity and preparedness rather than uncertainty or defensiveness

coaching_notes: This user has an unusually refined sense of narrative architecture in scientific writing — they think in terms of emotional arcs, not just information delivery. Their most consistent non-negotiable is that urgency must be *felt* by the reader, not merely stated; if a passage reads like a literature review or a factual catalogue, it has failed regardless of accuracy. They respond best when the assistant names specific craft choices and invites pressure-testing, and they are precise in distinguishing between what's 'working' and what's 'almost there.' When giving feedback, they often identify the exact sentence where momentum breaks — trust that specificity and revise surgically rather than rewriting broadly.

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is that urgency must be felt, not just named — if a reviewer could read a section without emotional engagement, it has failed regardless of factual accuracy. They consistently prefer meeting readers inside what they already know (clinical observations, familiar patterns) before introducing new mechanisms, treating this as both an accessibility strategy and a persuasive one. When evaluating drafts, they read aloud and trust gut reactions over analytical assessment — if a line 'deflates' or 'stops building,' the diagnosis is structural even when the content is correct.

When evaluating drafts, they read aloud and trust gut reactions over analytical assessment — if a line 'deflates' or 'stops building,' the diagnosis is structural even when the content is correct.

user_goals_summary: This user writes grant proposals that build narrative momentum and emotional urgency so the proposed work arrives as the only logical response to a pressurized problem. They value felt stakes over encyclopedic coverage, explicit naming of what the proposed work displaces, team capabilities framed as convergent readiness rather than credentials, aim independence, and interdisciplinary accessibility achieved by meeting readers inside clinical patterns they already recognize rather than front-loading definitions.

rubric.name: Escalating Urgency
rubric.description: Each sentence should raise the stakes beyond the previous one, building vertical pressure rather than cataloguing facts at equal weight and temperature. The gap must be felt, not just named. By the time the proposed work appears, it should feel like the only logical response — and the closing line must land as an earned arrival, not a summary or announcement. Mechanism and explanation may appear within the build, but only when they sharpen rather than relieve the pressure.

dimensions.label: Each successive sentence escalates stakes or narrows the problem rather than restating at the same level or temperature
The problem is rendered in terms that produce emotional engagement — the reader feels the human cost, not just the research gap
The solution or proposed work is withheld until sufficient pressure has been built — tension is not released by pivoting to methodology too early
The closing line of the urgency arc feels like an arrival that was built toward, not an announcement, summary, or tagline — it completes the story rather than declaring a new one
Sentence length, cadence, and structure are deliberately varied to sustain momentum — no run of same-length declarative sentences that lets the reader's attention relax

rubric.name: Explicit Contrast and Displacement
rubric.description: The writing must name the specific incumbent approach or clinical workaround currently occupying the space the proposed work will fill, and articulate why it falls short as a structural or design flaw rather than a resource problem. Contrasts must be named and landed, not gestured at or implied. Existing literature and prior approaches should be reframed as inputs to the proposed framework rather than competitors with it.

dimensions.label: The specific existing approach, instrument, or workaround currently occupying the clinical or research slot is named concretely — not left as an abstraction
The incumbent's limitation is framed as a design or structural flaw, not merely a resource, effort, or funding gap
The difference between the proposed approach and the incumbent is stated explicitly so the reader understands the substitution, not left for inference
Existing literature and prior approaches are reframed as inputs to or foundations for the proposed work rather than as competitors or failures

rubric.name: Interdisciplinary Accessibility
rubric.description: Writing must be legible to reviewers from different disciplinary backgrounds without sacrificing scientific precision. Accessibility is achieved by meeting readers inside clinical observations and familiar patterns they already recognize, then introducing mechanistic or technical concepts as the explanation those patterns have been waiting for. Specific names of instruments, signals, and protocols sharpen comprehension rather than hindering it, but discipline-specific terminology needs brief contextual cues so no reviewer loses the thread.

dimensions.label: Complex mechanisms are introduced through clinical observations or patterns the audience already recognizes — the reader's existing knowledge serves as the entry point before new frameworks are layered on
Discipline-specific terms are accompanied by brief parenthetical or contextual cues that allow non-specialist reviewers to continue reading without losing the thread
Key arguments address concerns that matter to different reviewer backgrounds — e.g., feasibility for engineers, clinical relevance for clinicians, population impact for public health
Concrete names of instruments, protocols, or signals are used in place of vague references, because specificity aids rather than hinders comprehension
Mechanistic claims are grounded in enough context that the reader understands why they matter — the science is earned rather than asking reviewers to trust it on authority

rubric.name: Team Positioning as Convergent Readiness
rubric.description: The team's preliminary work and capabilities should be framed as a launching position — a specific convergence of assets that makes this study possible now, for this team, in a way that could not have been true before. Past work is not a credential or a resume entry; it is the reason the proposed leap is credible. The team narrative should emerge from the logic of the science rather than appearing as a separate personnel section.

dimensions.label: Preliminary work is framed as what enables the proposed leap rather than as evidence of general competence — the focus is on what the team can now do, not what they have done
Multiple streams of prior work or complementary expertise are presented as arriving at the same threshold at the same time, creating a moment of convergence
The team's position is framed as specific and non-replicable — another team could not reach this threshold by starting fresh, because it depends on assets already built
Investigator roles and contributions are woven into the scientific argument so they feel like natural evidence of feasibility, not a personnel list appended to the science

rubric.name: Structural Resilience of Aims
rubric.description: Each aim should feel like a complete thought that produces a usable artifact — a framework, methodology, or characterized dataset — with independent value, not just a setup for subsequent aims. The relationship between aims should be one of building and refinement, not hard dependency. Language about contingencies should convey intellectual flexibility and preparedness, not defensiveness.

dimensions.label: Each aim explicitly names a deliverable that has independent scientific or clinical value regardless of outcomes in other aims
The primary deliverable is framed as a reusable framework or methodology, not solely as a binary finding that either works or doesn't
The aim's value is decoupled from any single component's performance — it retains value even if individual elements underperform
Language about contingencies and adaptability conveys intellectual maturity and preparedness rather than uncertainty or defensiveness

coaching_notes: This user thinks about grant writing as narrative craft, not information delivery — they will always prefer felt stakes over comprehensive coverage and will push back when sections read as 'encyclopedic' or 'literature review.' Their strongest and most consistent instinct is that urgency must be built vertically (each sentence escalating) rather than horizontally (facts arrayed at equal weight), and that closing lines must earn their landing through everything that preceded them. When incorporating team credentials or preliminary data, frame capabilities as convergent readiness at a specific threshold — the user rejects 'résumé language' and responds strongly to framing that makes the proposed work feel like the inevitable next step only this team could take.

user_goals_summary: This user writes grant proposals for mixed review panels and prioritizes emotional momentum over comprehensiveness. They want significance sections that make reviewers feel the problem so acutely that the proposed work arrives as the only logical response, and specific aims that demonstrate structural independence and team readiness. They consistently reject prose that catalogs facts at equal weight, closes with announcements rather than arrivals, or frames investigator credentials as résumé entries rather than evidence of convergent readiness.

rubric.name: Escalating Urgency
rubric.description: Each sentence should raise the stakes beyond the previous one, building vertical pressure rather than cataloguing facts at equal weight. The gap must be felt, not just named. The moment a paragraph begins accumulating information at the same temperature or reading like a literature review, the urgency collapses and the reader's attention relaxes.

rubric.name: Earned Arrival of Proposed Work
rubric.description: The proposed work must land as the inevitable, only logical response to the problem narrative that preceded it — not as an announcement, summary, or tagline. The closing should feel like an arrival the reader has been pulled toward, maintaining the emotional register of the preceding argument rather than collapsing into proposal-speak or methodology vocabulary. The reader should think 'why hasn't anyone done this already?' not 'oh, they're describing their project now.'

dimensions.label: The proposed work arrives as the only logical response to the problem narrative — the reader feels it was built toward, not declared
The closing language maintains the emotional and narrative register of the preceding argument rather than collapsing into process language, methodology vocabulary, or proposal-speak
The closing line connects back to images, language, or themes established earlier in the section, creating architectural intentionality rather than accumulation
The final framing maintains connection to the patients or communities at stake rather than pivoting entirely to the science or the team

Concrete names of instruments, protocols, or signals are used because specificity aids comprehension — vague references are replaced with named examples
Mechanistic claims are grounded in enough context that the reader understands why they matter — the science is earned rather than asserted on authority

rubric.description: Investigator credentials must be framed as evidence of readiness and convergent positioning, not as résumé entries. The prior work should feel like it was building toward this exact moment — a launching position that makes this study possible now, for this team, in a way that couldn't have been true before. Roles should emerge from the scientific argument rather than appearing as a separate personnel section.

coaching_notes: This user's primary non-negotiable is emotional momentum — they will reject technically accurate prose that reads as a literature review or catalog of facts, no matter how comprehensive. They consistently iterate on closing lines across multiple rounds and will push back repeatedly until the landing feels earned rather than announced; this is the single craft element they care most about getting right. They value restraint in how the field's prior work is critiqued: indictments should be structural and design-level, never personal, and should position existing research as inputs rather than failures — a stance that reflects both strategic reviewer management and genuine respect for the literature.
